interview_name: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett

main_summary.summary: Joint oral history with Emmett W. Bassett (b. Henry County, Virginia) and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett (b. Plainfield, New Jersey), recorded July 21, 2011 in Grahamsville, New York by Joe Mosnier with John Bishop. The couple, married 62 years at the time of recording, trace parallel paths into civil rights work: Priscilla's progressive-party engagement at Smith College and Life Magazine; Emmett's youth on a Black family farm adjacent to Bassett Furniture Industries, where his grandfather William Henry was displaced from a sawmill supervisory role under Virginia's racial restrictions. They met during a 1940s Northampton-Amherst roadhouse desegregation picket while Emmett was at the University of Massachusetts. Emmett describes Tuskegee work-study under Dr. George Washington Carver, military service in the segregated Quartermaster Corps at Camp Lee, his Ohio State University PhD in dairy technology (cited as a first for an African American), and the Chicago dairy industry's refusal to hire him. Priscilla describes UAW shop-stewardship at General Motors in Columbus, FBI Red Scare visits during Emmett's Ohio State University Hospital faculty consideration, In Friendship coalition work with Bayard Rustin and Stanley Levison on the 1963 March on Washington logistics, and her later work with the Council on Interracial Books for Children.
key_themes: Black agricultural and industrial labor history
FBI Red Scare and academic blacklisting
post-Brown school integration in New York City
main_summary.historical_significance:
The Bassetts' joint testimony documents a rarely-recorded class of mid-twentieth-century civil rights actors: scientific and library professionals whose activism unfolded through union locals, NAACP youth fundraising for the Emmett Till family, NYC public-school integration campaigns (Washington Heights middle-school integration, Ocean Hill-Brownsville community-control), and In Friendship coalition planning that fed into the 1963 March on Washington. Emmett's first-person account of pre-1965 Black voter registration in Henry County, Virginia—including the Jim Mullins coaching incident at age 19-20—is a primary-source record of how Black Virginians navigated the Whittle-era registrar discretion before the Voting Rights Act.

The interview also preserves Emmett's account of Bassett Furniture Industries' origins and his grandfather William Henry's displacement when the company sought $6 million from Continental Bank of Chicago contingent on Virginia state approval that required removing Black supervisors. Priscilla's account of refusing FBI cooperation in connection with the Women's International League for Peace and Freedom membership, and her resulting loss of an Ohio State University faculty consideration for Emmett, documents the academic-civil-liberties cost of Cold War security screening on integrated couples.

chapters.title: Priscilla's Plainfield and Smith Years
chapters.summary: Priscilla Bassett describes her Plainfield, New Jersey elementary education in a New Deal-enlightened environment with Harold Rugg textbooks, her Republican family's reaction to Franklin Delano Roosevelt's 1944 death, her work at Life Magazine after her father lost his job, and her engagement with the progressive party at Smith College. She mentions visiting Senator Clifford P. Case and her interest in Countee Cullen.

chapters.title: Emmett's Henry County Childhood
chapters.summary: Emmett describes his Henry County, Virginia family: his father's 1935 death when Emmett was 14, his half-brothers' education at Hampton Institute and St. Paul, his teacher Ida Dotson promoting him from fifth to seventh grade at the Chestnut Knob school, and the Bassett Furniture Industries' role in displacing his grandfather William Henry from a sawmill supervisory position under Virginia's racial restrictions on Black supervisors.
related_events: Continental Bank of Chicago furniture financing

chapters.title: Family Land and the Swimming Pond
chapters.summary: Emmett recounts the Tillesta furniture-company family that employed his father, the cross-racial cousinship between his mother and white neighbors that became known to him through a swimming pond his family built, and his post-University of Massachusetts visit to a white cousin of his father whom he refused to call on. Sets up the intergenerational interracial-kinship context of his later marriage.

chapters.title: Roadhouse Picket and Meeting
chapters.summary: Priscilla and Emmett describe meeting during a late-1940s civil rights picket of a segregated roadhouse on the Smith College-Amherst road. Priscilla served as a witness at the resulting trial. Emmett was studying at the University of Massachusetts. They describe their early relationship, including a Smith house mother's reaction and Plainfield public school memories of Paul Robeson performing.

chapters.title: Tuskegee and George Washington Carver
chapters.summary: Emmett describes his Tuskegee work-study at four o'clock in the morning milking 13 Guernsey cows for 15 to 20 cents an hour, his National Youth Administration scholarship arranged through writer Ralph Ellison, and his work at Dr. George Washington Carver's place for twelve hours a week showing visitors. Describes Carver's personal interactions including the speculation that Carver had been castrated as a young child.

chapters.title: Draft Board and Military Service
chapters.summary: Emmett describes refusing the Tuskegee Army Air Force volunteer route on his mother's advice, his draft-board exchange with judge Whittle in Martinsville, Virginia, his service in the Quartermaster Corps Company E at Camp Lee with Black officers including Colonel Snowden, his work with attorney Robert Ming, and his refusal to be moved to the back of an all-white meeting room. Includes Petersburg, Virginia incident and Fort A.P. Hill, Virginia training.

chapters.title: Sharecropper-Training Refusal
chapters.summary: After the war, Emmett worked as a county agricultural teacher in Henry County, Virginia for ninety dollars a month. He was offered a position teaching a Washington Virginia plantation school that would have trained returning Black veterans as sharecroppers receiving seventy-five dollars a month. Emmett refused, identifying the trap of veterans being stuck on plantations after the three-year federal subsidy expired. The encounter pushed him toward graduate study.
related_events: post-WWII Black veteran agricultural extension programs

chapters.title: Columbus, UAW, and Emmett Till Fundraising
chapters.summary: After the couple's marriage and brief New York period, they moved to Columbus while Emmett pursued his Ohio State University PhD. Priscilla worked at General Motors on the production line and became a UAW shop steward—she notes she was the only woman steward in that plant. She describes meeting a Black co-worker who had to work in the washroom on probation despite Curtiss-Wright wartime factory experience, then becoming active in the union. The chapter covers the Brown II decree, the late summer 1955 Till murder, and the Bassetts raising three thousand dollars for Mamie Till at a Columbus Baptist church.

chapters.title: Dairy Technology PhD and Industry Blacklisting
chapters.summary: Emmett describes his Ohio State University PhD as the first African American to take a doctorate in dairy technology. Chicago dairy companies including Beatrice Foods refused to hire him, with one executive saying the top would come off the building if a Black PhD were hired. Southern Black colleges in Florida and Georgia offered positions Emmett could not accept under Virginia miscegenation laws prior to Loving v. Virginia 1967. The chapter also covers his postdoctoral work at Columbia University on biosynthesis of patulin and aromatic amino acids in collaboration with Rockefeller Institute researchers.

chapters.title: Red Scare FBI Visits
chapters.summary: Priscilla describes her Women's International League for Peace and Freedom membership in Columbus and the FBI visit during Emmett's consideration for an Ohio State University assistant professorship under Dr. Gould. Dr. Gould reported FBI concerns about Priscilla's friends, including a woman who had been to Iowa House and whose husband was at the Ohio State University Hospital medical school. Emmett refused to talk to the FBI or CIA and lost the position. Priscilla also received an FBI visit while hanging clothes in the yard and declined to speak with them.
keywords: Women's International League for Peace and Freedom
related_events: Association of University Professors censure of Ohio State

chapters.title: School Integration in Washington Heights
chapters.summary: After moving to New York City, the Bassetts faced housing discrimination (a landlord changed the lock on an apartment they had claimed). Priscilla met Mae Mallory, who had worked with Robert F. Williams in North Carolina, at a New York City Public School System meeting. They sent their three children to the Fieldston School in Riverdale but Priscilla and Emmett also organized for the integration of three Washington Heights junior high schools, the Freedom School at a Methodist church, and Ocean Hill-Brownsville community control. Their car gas tank was filled with sugar by opponents.

chapters.title: In Friendship and March on Washington
chapters.summary: Priscilla describes her work with In Friendship, the 1956-era coalition organized by Stanley Levison and Bayard Rustin in support of Martin Luther King. Bayard Rustin had gone to Africa to oppose the French bomb when she joined. At an Apollo Theater meeting, In Friendship turned the March on Washington organizing over to A. Philip Randolph once the hundred-thousand-person threshold was reached. The chapter also covers the 1957 Prayer Pilgrimage for Freedom at the reflecting pool—the first time Priscilla heard Dr. King speak.

chapters.title: Malcolm X and the Mitchell-Lama Co-op
chapters.summary: Emmett describes attending three or four Malcolm X speeches in Harlem and a sidewalk conversation between Michael Harrington, Malcolm X, and himself. The Bassetts moved from a Mitchell-Lama cooperative in 1961 after the cooperative board voted to refuse meeting space for Upper Manhattan Committee for Equal Rights gatherings. They went to Sweden in 1964-65.
keywords: Upper Manhattan Committee for Equal Rights

chapters.title: Ortho Pharmaceutical and New Jersey College of Medicine
chapters.summary: After Sweden, Emmett worked two years at Ortho Pharmaceutical (a Johnson and Johnson subsidiary) but left over concerns including the company's practice of buying blood from poor communities through front groups. He was recruited to the New Jersey College of Medicine and Dentistry after the Newark rebellion when the institution had no African American faculty; he served on the admissions committee. Johnson and Johnson countered with offers including free tuition for his daughter but he refused to return.
keywords: New Jersey College of Medicine and Dentistry

chapters.title: Uppsala and Reflections on King and Malcolm
chapters.summary: The Bassetts describe their year at Uppsala, Sweden where Emmett worked in Dr. Arne Tiselius's laboratory after recommendation from Columbia colleagues. They traveled by ocean liner to Norway, visited a Rockefeller Institute friend in Trondheim, Norway, and were welcomed by a Swedish station master who took them into his house overnight. Priscilla reflects on Dr. King's Riverside Church speech, the Vietnam war's wrongness following Dien Bien Phu, Muhammad Ali's draft refusal statement, and her respect for both King and Malcolm X following Manning Marable's biography.

chapters.title: Interracial Marriage and Loving v. Virginia
chapters.summary: Priscilla reflects on the couple's 62-year marriage. She recalls an NAACP statement (attributed in the transcript to Dr. Du Bois though this attribution is chronologically uncertain) following Loving v. Virginia that the NAACP did not oppose or support interracial marriage but believed individuals who fall in love should pursue their relationships. She emphasizes respecting cultural difference rather than racial elimination by consolidation. Their older daughter attributed their long marriage to a shared moral compass.

chapters.title: Human Rights Commission and Voter Registration
chapters.summary: Emmett describes his service on the New York City Human Rights Commission, including monthly meetings at the Plaza Hotel funded by the governor, monitoring housing-organizing groups in his community. The interview closes with his account of registering to vote in Henry County, Virginia around 1942 or 1943 before entering the Army: at the Field Station two miles from home he was handed a blank sheet of paper rather than an application, was coached the previous week by Jim Mullins (a Black businessman who ran the cornmeal mill), and was registered after adding Negro to the form.
related_events: pre-1965 Black voter registration in Virginia
NYC Human Rights Commission housing oversight

loc_video.caption: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett oral history interview conducted by Joseph Mosnier in Grahamsville, New York, 2011 July 21
